title: Roast Duck with Orange
subtitle: Crispy-skinned duck with a bitter orange sauce
excerpt: Duck has more fat than chicken and needs a different roasting strategy: a long initial phase to render it, then a blast to crisp the skin. The bitter orange sauce is the classic British-French companion. Prick the skin liberally before it goes in, drain off fat twice during cooking, and plan for a richer, darker meal than a standard Sunday roast.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: roast duck with orange sauce. Classic French canard à l'orange adapted for British home cooking. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): duck and orange sauce method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The orange sauce can be made a day ahead and reheated gently. The duck is best roasted and served immediately.

glossaryTerms.definition: Making small holes in duck or goose skin with a fork or skewer before roasting, allowing subcutaneous fat to drain into the pan rather than remaining under the skin and softening it.

content.text: A whole duck feeds four, barely. The bird is large but its breast meat is dense, and the thighs and drumsticks are smaller than chicken. What it lacks in volume it makes up for in depth of flavour: the dark, iron-rich breast meat against the acidity of the orange sauce is one of the more satisfying combinations on the British table.
The challenge with duck is its exceptional fat content.

all over before it goes in and draining accumulated fat twice during cooking are both necessary steps. The duck needs about 2 hours total at 200°C (180°C fan), finishing with a 220°C blast for the final 15 minutes to crisp the skin.

A rack inside the roasting pan is not optional for duck: without it the bird sits in its own rendered fat for two hours and the underside turns soft. The fat that drains into the pan is excellent for roasting potatoes; keep it.

Pat the duck thoroughly dry inside and out. Prick the skin all over with a fork or skewer, concentrating on the breast and thighs where the fat layer is thickest. Make 30 to 40 holes, going through the skin but not into the meat. Season all over with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}, including the cavity. Rub the orange zest into the skin. Push the thyme sprigs and halved orange pieces into the cavity.

Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Place the duck breast-side up on the rack in the roasting pan. Roast for 1 hour. Carefully pour off the accumulated fat into a jar and reserve for later use. Return the duck and roast for a further 45 minutes. Drain the fat again. Raise the oven to 220°C (200°C fan) and roast for a final 15 minutes until the skin is deep golden-brown. The thigh should read 75°C to 80°C on a probe.

While the duck rests, make the sauce. Melt {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar in a dry medium saucepan over medium heat without stirring. When it turns a deep amber caramel, add {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar carefully (it will spit). Stir until the caramel dissolves. Add the juice of 4 oranges and {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Simmer for 8 minutes until slightly syrupy. Season and whisk in {{unsalted-butter}} of butter off the heat to gloss the sauce.

Rest the duck for 15 minutes under loose foil. To carve: remove both legs at the hip joint, then slice the breast off the breastbone in one piece per side and cut on the diagonal. Two legs and half a breast per person is the standard serving.

rows.symptom: Skin soft and fatty rather than crisp
rows.cause: Insufficient pricking or fat not drained during cooking
rows.fix: Prick more aggressively before the next attempt and drain fat twice during cooking. The accumulated fat must leave the pan.

rows.symptom: Breast meat dry while legs are still underdone
rows.cause: Duck anatomy: the breast is thinner and the leg joint thick
rows.fix: Remove the breast from the oven 5 minutes before the legs if the breast reads 75°C early. The legs can continue.

rows.cause: Caramel taken too dark, or orange pith included in the juice
rows.fix: Balance with a teaspoon of honey and reduce for a further 2 minutes. Use a juicer that does not extract pith.

content.text: Duck with orange is an Anglo-French classic that arrived in British cookery via the eighteenth-century French influence on British upper-class tables. Mrs Beeton includes a version of the orange sauce in the 1861 edition. The dish reached its British peak in the 1970s restaurant boom and spent the 1990s in mild unfashionability. It has since been rehabilitated as a dinner party main that is less demanding than its reputation suggests. Serve with roast potatoes cooked in the reserved duck fat and a watercress salad.
